grammarItems.title: Hangul syllable blocks
grammarItems.plainEnglish: Korean is written in syllable blocks. Each block usually has an initial consonant, a vowel, and sometimes a final consonant.
grammarItems.whenToUse: Study this before memorizing many words so you can read Korean from Hangul instead of depending on romanization.
grammarItems.attachmentRule: Read each block left-to-right or top-to-bottom inside the square: 한 = ㅎ + ㅏ + ㄴ, 글 = ㄱ + ㅡ + ㄹ.
grammarItems.contrastWith: Romanization is only a temporary pronunciation helper. It is not the real Korean spelling.
commonMistakes: Do not pronounce romanization as English spelling.
Do not pause between every syllable block when speaking.
grammarItems.beginnerExplanation: For a beginner, the key is not memorizing every sound rule at once. The key is seeing that Korean is built from reusable letters inside syllable blocks. Read the block first, then listen.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

grammarItems.title: Topic and contrast
grammarItems.plainEnglish: Use this to say what the sentence is about: “as for...” or “when we talk about this...”
grammarItems.whenToUse: Use 은/는 when introducing yourself, setting the topic, comparing two things, or changing the topic.
grammarItems.attachmentRule: Use 은 after a final consonant and 는 after a vowel: 학생은, 저는.
grammarItems.contrastWith: 이/가 points to the subject or new information. 은/는 sets the topic or contrast.

examples.en: As for me, I am a student.
As for today, I cannot go.

commonMistakes: Do not translate 은/는 every time. It often just marks the topic.
Do not stack 도 after 은/는 as 저는도; say 저도.
grammarItems.beginnerExplanation: Think of 은/는 as putting a label on the sentence topic. It tells the listener, “This is what I am talking about now.” It often creates contrast, even when English does not show it.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

grammarItems.title: Subject and new information
grammarItems.plainEnglish: Use this to mark the subject that exists, appears, hurts, or is being identified.
grammarItems.whenToUse: Use 이/가 with 있어요, 없어요, 아파요, 좋아요, and when the subject is new or important.
grammarItems.attachmentRule: Use 이 after a final consonant and 가 after a vowel: 시간이, 친구가.
grammarItems.contrastWith: 은/는 is the topic. 이/가 points to the subject inside the sentence.

examples.en: I have time. / There is time.

commonMistakes: Do not use 을/를 with 있어요 or 없어요 for the thing that exists.
Memorize common chunks first: 시간이 있어요, 문제가 없어요, 머리가 아파요.
grammarItems.beginnerExplanation: Think of 이/가 as pointing to the subject that appears, exists, hurts, or is newly important. It often answers “who?” or “what?” inside the sentence.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

grammarItems.title: Object marker
grammarItems.plainEnglish: Use this after the thing that receives the action.
grammarItems.whenToUse: Use 을/를 with verbs like eat, drink, buy, read, write, study, watch, and take.
grammarItems.attachmentRule: Use 을 after a final consonant and 를 after a vowel: 밥을, 커피를.
grammarItems.contrastWith: 이/가 marks the subject. 을/를 marks the object of an action.
commonMistakes: Koreans often drop 을/를 in speech, but learners should understand it first.
Do not put 을/를 after places with 가요; say 학교에 가요.
grammarItems.beginnerExplanation: 을/를 marks the thing affected by an action. If you eat, drink, buy, read, write, or study something, that something often takes 을/를.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

grammarItems.title: Time, destination, and existence place
grammarItems.plainEnglish: Use this for “to,” “at,” “in,” or “on” when the noun is a destination, time, or place where something exists.
grammarItems.whenToUse: Use 에 with 가요/와요 for destination, with times like 7시에, and with 있어요/없어요 for location.
grammarItems.attachmentRule: Attach 에 directly after the noun: 학교에, 집에, 7시에.
grammarItems.contrastWith: 에서 is where an action happens. 에 is the destination or existence location.
commonMistakes: For destination, use 에: 학교에 가요.
For the place of an action, use 에서: 학교에서 공부해요.
grammarItems.beginnerExplanation: 에 is a target marker: a destination, a time point, or the place where something exists. It does not usually mark where an active action happens.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

grammarItems.title: Action place and starting point
grammarItems.plainEnglish: Use this for the place where an action happens, or the place something starts from.
grammarItems.whenToUse: Use 에서 with study, work, eat, wait, buy, meet, and with came from.
grammarItems.attachmentRule: Attach 에서 after the place: 학교에서, 회사에서, 서울에서.
grammarItems.contrastWith: 에 is destination or existence place. 에서 is action place.
commonMistakes: Do not say 학교에서 가요 for destination. Say 학교에 가요.
Use 에서 only when an action is happening there or something starts there.
grammarItems.beginnerExplanation: 에서 is an action-place marker. If something happens at a place, use 에서. This is why 학교에서 공부해요 is different from 학교에 가요.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

grammarItems.title: To a person
grammarItems.plainEnglish: Use this when a person receives an item, message, action, or question.
grammarItems.whenToUse: Use 에게 in neutral/written Korean, 한테 in everyday speech, and 께 for honorific speech.
grammarItems.attachmentRule: Attach directly after the person: 친구에게, 저한테, 선생님께.
grammarItems.contrastWith: Use 에 for places. Use 에게/한테 for people or animals.

miniDialogue.speaker: A
miniDialogue.en: Who do I give it to?

examples.en: I give a gift to a friend.

commonMistakes: Do not use 에게 for ordinary destinations. Say 학교에 가요, not 학교에게 가요.
Use 께 with respected people when you want honorific direction.

practiceItems.prompt: I give a gift to a friend.

grammarItems.beginnerExplanation: Use 에게 or 한테 when the receiver is a person. This is about direction toward a person, not physical travel to a place.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

grammarItems.title: Method, direction, and language
grammarItems.plainEnglish: Use this for by, with, toward, or in a language.
grammarItems.whenToUse: Use 로/으로 for transportation, payment, tools, direction, and language.
grammarItems.attachmentRule: Use 로 after a vowel or ㄹ ending. Use 으로 after most consonants: 카드로, 펜으로.
grammarItems.contrastWith: 을/를 marks the object. 로/으로 marks the method or tool.
commonMistakes: 카드로 사요 means buy by card, not buy a card.
Do not use 로/으로 for “with a friend”; use 친구하고 or 친구랑 for that meaning.
Do not confuse 로/으로 with destination 에.
grammarItems.beginnerExplanation: 로/으로 often answers “by what method?” or “with what tool?” It can also show direction or language. In shopping, 카드로 is “by card.”
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

grammarItems.title: Also / too / even
grammarItems.plainEnglish: Use this to add one more thing to the same idea.
grammarItems.whenToUse: Use 도 for me too, this too, today too, and similar additions.
grammarItems.attachmentRule: Attach 도 after the word you are adding: 저도, 이것도, 오늘도.
grammarItems.contrastWith: 도 often replaces 은/는, 이/가, or 을/를 in that position.
commonMistakes: Say 저도, not 저는도.
도 changes focus, so place it after the exact word you are adding.
grammarItems.beginnerExplanation: 도 replaces the particle you might expect and means “also/too/even.” 저도 means “me too,” not 저는도.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

grammarItems.title: Only / just
grammarItems.plainEnglish: Use this to limit the sentence to one thing.
grammarItems.whenToUse: Use 만 for only this, only water, only today, or just one thing.
grammarItems.attachmentRule: Attach 만 after the word you want to limit: 물만, 오늘만, 이것만.
grammarItems.contrastWith: 도 adds another thing. 만 limits the sentence to one thing.
commonMistakes: 만 changes focus. 물만 마셔요 means only water, not only drinking.
Place 만 after the word you want to limit.
grammarItems.beginnerExplanation: 만 narrows the sentence to “only this.” In shopping, 이것만 주세요 means you do not want the other items.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

grammarItems.title: From / until
grammarItems.plainEnglish: Use these to mark the start and end of a time or place range.
grammarItems.whenToUse: Use 부터/까지 with schedules, opening hours, travel routes, and time ranges.
grammarItems.attachmentRule: Use 부터 for the start and 까지 for the end: 9시부터 6시까지.
grammarItems.contrastWith: 에 marks one time or place. 부터/까지 marks a range.

miniDialogue.speaker: A
miniDialogue.en: From what time do you work?

miniDialogue.speaker: A
miniDialogue.en: I walk from here to the station.

examples.en: I study from Monday to Friday.
I walk from here to the station.

commonMistakes: 까지 can mean until for time or as far as for place.
Do not use only 부터 if the ending point is important.

practiceItems.prompt: I walk from here to the station.

grammarItems.beginnerExplanation: 부터 and 까지 create a range. They are useful for schedules, routes, and opening hours before you can make complex sentences.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

endingItems.title: Want to do
endingItems.plainEnglish: Add 고 싶어요 to a verb stem to say what you want to do.
endingItems.whenToUse: Use it for your own wants: eating, going, resting, learning, buying, or trying something.
endingItems.attachmentRule: Remove 다 from the dictionary form, then add 고 싶어요: 먹다 → 먹고 싶어요.
endingItems.contrastWith: Use N 주세요 to ask for an item. Use V고 싶어요 to talk about your desire.
commonMistakes: Do not use 고 싶어요 for adjectives like pretty or expensive.
For another person's wants, beginners can first quote or ask directly instead of overbuilding grammar.
endingItems.beginnerExplanation: 고 싶어요 attaches to a verb stem and expresses the speaker’s desire. It is usually safer for “I want to do” than translating English word by word.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

endingItems.title: Can do / possible
endingItems.plainEnglish: Use this to say an action is possible because of skill, time, permission-like possibility, or circumstances.
endingItems.whenToUse: Use it for ability and practical possibility: can go, can eat, can read, can pay by card.
endingItems.attachmentRule: Use ㄹ 수 있어요 after a vowel-ending stem and 을 수 있어요 after a consonant-ending stem.
endingItems.contrastWith: 못 + verb is the shorter everyday way to say cannot.
commonMistakes: Do not use this for simple preference; use 고 싶어요 for want.
For quick speech, 카드 돼요? is a natural shortcut in stores.
endingItems.beginnerExplanation: ㄹ/을 수 있어요 means the action is possible. The ㄹ/을 part depends on the verb stem ending, so memorize complete examples first.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

endingItems.title: Cannot / unable to
endingItems.plainEnglish: Put 못 before a verb to say you cannot do it because of ability, schedule, rules, health, or circumstances.
endingItems.whenToUse: Use it for real limits: schedule conflicts, food restrictions, language ability, health, rules, or practical impossibility.
endingItems.attachmentRule: For most verbs, put 못 before the normal polite verb. For 하다 verbs, use 못 해요 or noun + 못 해요.
endingItems.contrastWith: 안 means you do not do something. 못 means you cannot do it.
commonMistakes: Say 못 가요, not 못 가해요.
For 하다 verbs, use 못 해요 or noun + 못 해요: 공부 못 해요.
endingItems.beginnerExplanation: 못 is the inability negative. It says a real limit blocks the action. For most verbs, it simply goes before the verb: 못 가요, 못 먹어요.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

endingItems.title: Have to / must
endingItems.plainEnglish: Use this to say an action is necessary or required.
endingItems.whenToUse: Use it for schedules, rules, health needs, work, school, and personal obligations.
endingItems.attachmentRule: Use the 아/어 form of the verb, then add 야 해요: 가다 → 가야 해요, 먹다 → 먹어야 해요.
endingItems.contrastWith: V지 않아도 돼요 means the action is not necessary.

examples.en: I have to go early tomorrow.
I have a test, so I have to study.

commonMistakes: Do not use this for a simple future plan; it sounds like obligation.
하다 verbs become 해야 해요.
endingItems.beginnerExplanation: 아/어야 해요 creates obligation. It often sounds like “I have to,” not just “I will.” Use it for rules, health, schedules, and necessary tasks.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

endingItems.title: May I / is it okay to do?
endingItems.plainEnglish: Use this to ask whether an action is allowed or okay.
endingItems.whenToUse: Use it before sitting, entering, taking photos, using a card, or doing anything where permission matters.
endingItems.attachmentRule: Use the 아/어 form of the verb, then add 도 돼요?
endingItems.contrastWith: V지 마세요 tells someone not to do something.
commonMistakes: Use a question intonation when asking permission.
Do not confuse 돼요 with 되어요 spelling; 돼요 is the common form.

practiceItems.prompt: 사진 찍다 → may I take a photo?
카드 쓰다 → may I use a card?

endingItems.beginnerExplanation: 아/어도 돼요? asks whether doing an action is okay. This is essential in travel, classrooms, restaurants, and public places.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

endingItems.title: Please do
endingItems.plainEnglish: Use this to politely ask someone to do an action.
endingItems.whenToUse: Use it for help, opening, repeating, taking a photo, checking something, or saying something again.
endingItems.attachmentRule: Use the 아/어 form of the verb, then add 주세요.
endingItems.contrastWith: N 주세요 asks for an item. V아/어 주세요 asks someone to do an action.
commonMistakes: Do not use this for your own action; it asks someone else to act.
Add 좀 to soften many everyday requests.
endingItems.beginnerExplanation: 아/어 주세요 asks someone else to do an action. It is different from N 주세요, which asks for an item.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

endingItems.title: Please do not
endingItems.plainEnglish: Use this for polite prohibitions, warnings, signs, and careful requests.
endingItems.whenToUse: Use it when telling someone not to go, not to take photos, not to open something, or not to worry.
endingItems.attachmentRule: Remove 다 from the dictionary form, then add 지 마세요.
endingItems.contrastWith: 안 V해요 says someone does not do an action. V지 마세요 tells someone not to do it.

examples.en: Please do not take photos here.

commonMistakes: This can sound like an instruction, so use it carefully with people.
You will often read it on signs before you need to say it yourself.

practiceItems.prompt: 사진 찍다 → please do not take photos

endingItems.beginnerExplanation: 지 마세요 tells someone not to do something. Beginners should first recognize it on signs and staff instructions.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

endingItems.title: Because / so
endingItems.plainEnglish: Use this to connect a reason to a result.
endingItems.whenToUse: Use it for simple reasons: busy so cannot go, sick so rest, expensive so do not buy.
endingItems.attachmentRule: Use 아서 after many ㅏ/ㅗ stems and 어서 after many other stems. 하다 becomes 해서.
endingItems.contrastWith: 고 simply connects ideas; 아서/어서 explains why.

examples.en: I am busy, so I cannot go.
I am sick, so I rest.

commonMistakes: Avoid using 아서/어서 before commands in careful textbook grammar.
Keep the first clause short as a beginner.
endingItems.beginnerExplanation: 아서/어서 joins a reason and a result. Start with short sentences like 바빠서 못 가요 before using it in longer speech.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

endingItems.title: And / and then
endingItems.plainEnglish: Use 고 to connect two actions or descriptions.
endingItems.whenToUse: Use it for sequence or listing: eat and go, study and work, cheap and good.
endingItems.attachmentRule: Remove 다 from the first verb or adjective, then add 고.
endingItems.contrastWith: 아서/어서 gives a reason. 고 usually just connects.

examples.en: I eat and go to school.
This bag is cheap and good.

commonMistakes: 고 does not always mean a strict time order; context decides.
Do not make beginner sentences too long.
endingItems.beginnerExplanation: 고 connects actions or descriptions. It can mean “and” or “and then,” depending on context.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

endingItems.title: If / when
endingItems.plainEnglish: Use 면 to set a condition.
endingItems.whenToUse: Use it for time, price, health, permission, and simple advice.
endingItems.attachmentRule: Remove 다 from the verb or adjective, then add 면.
endingItems.contrastWith: 아서/어서 gives a reason that already exists. 면 gives a condition.

examples.en: If you have time, please call.
If it is expensive, I do not buy it.
If you are sick, you should rest.

commonMistakes: Context can make 면 sound like if or when.
Keep the condition short at first.
endingItems.beginnerExplanation: 면 creates a condition. Beginners can read it as “if,” but in real context it can also feel like “when.”
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.

endingItems.title: I will / speaker promise
endingItems.plainEnglish: Use this when the speaker decides or promises what they will do.
endingItems.whenToUse: Use it when volunteering, responding to someone, leaving, waiting, or promising a call.
endingItems.attachmentRule: Use ㄹ게요 after a vowel-ending stem and 을게요 after a consonant-ending stem.
endingItems.contrastWith: ㄹ/을 거예요 is a future plan. ㄹ/을게요 is the speaker's decision in response to the situation.
commonMistakes: Use this only for what the speaker will do.
Do not use it to predict another person's action.
endingItems.beginnerExplanation: ㄹ/을게요 is the speaker’s decision or promise. It is common when responding to someone: 제가 할게요.
studyOrder: Read the full example first.
Find the noun, verb stem, or adjective stem that changes.
Say the frame aloud with one substitution.
Check the common mistake before making a new sentence.
